text:
**"Read" in the spins = the *projection* step: turning the claim log into the guide `.md` files on disk.** That happens at **capture time** (or a periodic compaction pass) — the same moment capture runs today, which is *not* latency-sensitive.

The two moments, kept distinct:

| Moment | What happens | Latency sensitivity |
|---|---|---|
| **Capture / compaction** ("read" in the spins) | claims → rendered guide `.md` written to disk | Not hot — runs on SessionEnd / on a schedule |
| **Inject** (the SELECT + COMPILE pipeline) | reads the already-materialized guide `.md`, synthesizes a briefing into Claude's context | **Hot** — fires on every prompt |

So in all three spins, the projection produces **on-disk guide markdown**, exactly like the files you have now. **Inject is completely unchanged** — it reads those `.md` files via SELECT→COMPILE just as it does today, fast, with no idea how they were generated.

This is actually a load-bearing design rule worth stating explicitly: **you must never defer projection to inject-time.** If a "pure event-sourcing" reading tempted you to "project the guide on demand when inject needs it," you'd be dropping an expensive LLM render onto the pre-prompt hot path — the exact latency mistake we fixed earlier with the gate. The discipline is: **guides are always materialized to disk at capture/compaction; inject only ever reads materialized files.**

So when I called Spin 2's reads "expensive" — that cost is paid at **capture time** (every render passes the full claim history through an LLM), amortized off the hot path, *not* paid per user prompt. That's also why the test harness calls `project()` once to write `guides/*.md` and scores those files: it's modeling the capture-side projection, and inject isn't in the experiment at all.
